title: RBAC vs ABAC in Keycloak
description:
## When Roles Aren't Enough

A new requirement: access to the payroll endpoint must depend on the user's **`department` attribute** (`finance`) **and the time of day** (only during business hours) — not merely on whether they hold a role.

What is the appropriate Keycloak approach?

options.text: Use **Authorization Services policies** (ABAC-style): combine a **user/attribute or JS policy** that checks `department == finance` with a **time policy** for business hours, via an aggregate permission
Create more **roles** (e.g. `finance-9to5`) and assign them — roles alone cover any access requirement
Build one large **composite role** that encodes the department and the schedule
Move the check into the app and read the `department` claim manually, because Keycloak cannot evaluate attributes

explanation: Plain roles give you **RBAC** — decisions based on role membership. This requirement is **ABAC**: it depends on a user *attribute* (`department`) and *contextual* state (time of day). Keycloak's **Authorization Services** support this through policy types such as **user/attribute** and **JavaScript** policies (evaluate `department`) combined with a **time** policy, joined by an **aggregate** policy/permission. (a) is correct. (b) and (c) try to fake attributes and context by minting ever more roles or one giant composite — roles can't express 'during business hours' or read arbitrary attributes, so this explodes combinatorially and still can't capture time. (d) is false: Keycloak *can* evaluate attributes and context server-side; pushing it into the app re-creates the hardcoded logic ABAC was meant to remove.

hints.title: RBAC = role; ABAC = attributes + context
hints.body: If the rule reads user attributes, group membership, time, or request context, it's ABAC territory — model it with Authorization Services policies rather than inventing new roles.
